core_intuitions: Bare attention is permutation-equivariant: it sees an unordered SET of vectors, so 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' are identical to it. Word order carries no signal unless we inject position ourselves.

There are exactly two places to inject position: Approach A adds to the representation (x_i + p_i, before attention), Approach B adds a bias to the pre-softmax score S_ij. RoPE is the hybrid: it touches q,k (A's locus) but is engineered so q·k depends only on the relative offset (B's goal).

RoPE rotates each 2-D feature pair by an angle proportional to its position; when you dot a query rotated by mθ with a key rotated by nθ, the absolute angles cancel and only the relative offset (m−n)θ survives.

Position is encoded as a SPECTRUM of rotation frequencies: d/2 pairs on a geometric ladder from fast (local, wavelength ~6 tokens) to slow (long-range, wavelength ~50k tokens). This same ladder underlies sinusoidal encodings and RoPE.

Extrapolation fails because the slow pairs barely turn inside the trained window, so past L_train they rotate into angles the model never observed. Context extension = reshaping the frequency ladder so those slow-pair angles stay inside the trained range.

The extension lineage reshapes that ladder three ways: PI squeezes every dimension uniformly, NTK-aware stretches the base non-uniformly (fast dims untouched, slow dims stretched), YaRN adds a per-dimension ramp plus an attention-logit temperature.

existing.name: rope-rotation
existing.issue: The core geometry (a pair rotating by mθ, and the inset showing q·k depending on the angle difference) is correct and reads clearly in both themes — this is a genuinely good figure. Two small polish issues: in dark mode the phrase 'only offset matters' in the bottom-right box sits right against the box's right border (near-clip), and in the small 'Query m vs. key n' inset the 'relative position' caption crowds the horizontal axis line.
existing.improvement: Add a few px of right padding inside the bottom-right result box so 'only offset matters' clears the border in both themes; nudge the inset's 'angle difference = relative position' caption down (or the inset axis up) so the text no longer overlaps the axis. No structural change — the figure conveys its idea well and should be kept.

new_figures.name: rope-frequency-ladder
new_figures.concept: Position as a spectrum of rotation frequencies (fast local pairs vs slow long-range pairs) and why the slow pairs cause extrapolation failure past the trained length.
new_figures.rationale: This is the single most foundational missing intuition in the chapter. The frequency ladder is the shared skeleton of sinusoidal encoding, RoPE's d-dimensional lift, the 'how fast does each dimension spin' worked example, AND the entire context-extension section. Every scaling trick (PI/NTK/YaRN) is a manipulation of this ladder, so a reader who does not SEE the ladder and the 'slow dims run off into unseen angles' problem cannot understand the back half of the chapter. Currently it lives only in prose and one worked-example arithmetic block.
new_figures.placement: Inside the RoPE section, right after the worked-example admonition 'Worked example: how fast does each RoPE dimension spin?' (which computes θ_0=1 rad/tok and θ_63≈1e-4 rad/tok).
new_figures.brief: A wide two-part infographic. LEFT (~30% width): a vertical stack of 3 small clock dials labelled top-to-bottom 'fast pair k=0 (θ≈1 rad/tok, wavelength ~6 tokens)', 'mid pair', 'slow pair k=d/2−1 (θ≈1e-4 rad/tok, wavelength ~50k tokens)'. Each dial shows a single hand at the SAME token position: the fast dial's hand has swept most of the way around (many turns implied by a small spiral or '×many' tag), the mid dial's hand is at a moderate angle, the slow dial's hand is barely tilted off vertical. Caption under the stack: 'one position, d/2 hands spinning at geometrically-spaced rates.' RIGHT (~70%): a line plot, x-axis = token position (0 out to well past a marked L_train tick, e.g. label the tick '4K' as illustrative only), y-axis = rotation angle (radians, mark 0, π, 2π). Shade the vertical band [0, L_train] and label it 'trained window — angles the model has seen'; shade the region beyond and label it 'never seen in training'. Draw three curves colored to match the three dials: (1) fast pair — a steep line that wraps many times, drawn as a dense sawtooth (angle mod 2π) to make 'many full cycles even in short training' visible; (2) mid pair — a moderate line completing a couple of cycles; (3) slow pair — a nearly-flat line that inside the band rises to only a tiny fraction of one cycle (annotate '<8% of a turn at L_train'), then CONTINUES climbing into the 'never seen' region (annotate 'past L_train these angles are unfamiliar → RoPE degrades'). Add one summarizing callout arrow at the bottom: 'context extension = keep the slow-pair curve inside the trained band.' Theme-safe: use the theme's accent tokens for the three curves and a neutral hatch/desaturated emphasis token for the 'never seen' band (no hardcoded red). All integers illustrative; everything inside the viewBox with clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading order.

new_figures.name: rope-scaling-methods
new_figures.concept: PI vs NTK-aware vs YaRN as three distinct ways to reshape the RoPE frequency ladder so long positions stay within trained angles.
new_figures.rationale: The context-extension trio is the most interview-hot and most confusing material in the chapter, and it is delivered as three dense prose paragraphs plus formulas with no visual. The key intuition — that PI squeezes ALL dimensions equally (wasteful), NTK stretches NON-uniformly (fast dims kept, slow dims stretched), and YaRN adds a per-dimension RAMP plus a softmax-sharpening temperature — is inherently spatial (what happens across the ladder) and is exactly the kind of thing a single comparative diagram makes click. Builds directly on the frequency-ladder figure.
new_figures.placement: In the 'Context-Length Extension: Stretching RoPE to 128K' section, right after the paragraph introducing the 'three families of fixes ... instrument with many strings' metaphor and before the 'Position Interpolation (PI)' subsection.
new_figures.brief: Three stacked (or side-by-side, whichever fits legibly) panels that share one common motif: a horizontal 'frequency ladder' rendered as a row of ~8 rungs/dials running left=fast/local to right=slow/long-range, matching the color language of the rope-frequency-ladder figure. In each panel show, below or on each rung, an arrow or bar indicating how much that dimension is scaled. PANEL 1 'Position Interpolation (uniform squeeze)': every rung gets an identical inward-squeeze arrow of the same length; caption 'pos → pos/s (e.g. s=4): every dimension compressed equally — even the fast local dims that were never the problem lose resolution; usually needs a short fine-tune.' PANEL 2 'NTK-aware (stretch the base)': a graded response — leftmost (fast) rungs have near-zero arrows labelled 'untouched, local precision kept', arrows grow toward the right so the rightmost (slow) rungs have the largest stretch labelled 'stretched most'; caption 'base → base·s^(d/(d−2)): non-uniform; often training-free for 2–4×.' PANEL 3 'YaRN (per-dim ramp + temperature)': divide the ladder into three labelled regions — left segment 'short wavelength → untouched', right segment 'long wavelength → interpolated (PI-style)', middle segment drawn as a smooth ramp 'blend'; then off to the side a small companion icon of a softmax bar-distribution shown going from flat to peaked with the tag '×(0.1·ln s + 1) logit temperature — re-sharpens the flattened attention'; caption 'NTK-by-parts ramp + attention temperature: best reach, the production default.' BOTTOM: a single lineage arrow 'PI (uniform, needs fine-tune) → NTK-aware (non-uniform, often free) → YaRN (per-dim ramp + temperature)'. Theme-safe accent tokens only; s=4 and the 8 rungs are illustrative integers; keep all three panels inside the viewBox with no overlapping labels.

new_figures.name: position-injection-sites
new_figures.concept: The two sites where position can enter attention (Approach A: add to the representation; Approach B: add to the score) and RoPE as the hybrid that lives between them.
new_figures.rationale: This A/B fork is the explicit organizing spine of the whole chapter ('the entire chapter is organized around this fork'), but it is currently a plain-text ASCII box. A clean data-path infographic that pins each method to its injection site gives the reader a mental map they carry through every subsequent section, and it makes RoPE's 'hybrid' status — the chapter's central cleverness — visually obvious rather than asserted.
new_figures.placement: In the 'Why Attention Needs Position, and the Two Ways to Add It' section, replacing / upgrading the ASCII 'APPROACH A ... APPROACH B' text block.
new_figures.brief: A single left-to-right attention data path drawn once as a clean pipeline, with two clearly marked injection taps. Flow: token embeddings x_i → [Q,K,V projections] → score S_ij = (q_i·k_j)/√d_k → softmax → ·V → output. TAP A ('Approach A — add to the representation'): a downward arrow labelled '+ p_i' entering the x_i node BEFORE the projections; hang a small tag listing 'sinusoidal, learned absolute'. TAP B ('Approach B — add to the score'): a downward arrow labelled '+ bias(i,j)' entering the score node S_ij; tag 'T5 relative bias, ALiBi'. Then draw a visually distinct THIRD element for RoPE: a small 'rotate q,k' badge placed ON the Q and K lines between the projection and the dot product, with a callout box 'RoPE (hybrid): touches q,k like Approach A, but rotation makes q·k depend only on the offset i−j, achieving Approach B’s relative goal.' Include a compact legend keying three colors: A, B, RoPE-hybrid. Use the theme's accent tokens (color A, color B, and a highlight accent for RoPE); no hardcoded primaries. Enforce a clear single reading direction (left to right for the path, taps entering from above), keep every label legible and fully inside the viewBox.

summary: The chapter is well-written and concept-dense but figure-poor: a single existing figure (rope-rotation, good — keep with minor padding polish) serves only the RoPE relative-property intuition, while the chapter's other foundational ideas — the organizing A/B injection fork, the frequency-ladder spectrum, the slow-dim extrapolation failure, and the PI→NTK→YaRN reshaping lineage — are all text-only. I propose three new static figures: (1) rope-frequency-ladder [P1] visualizing the geometric spectrum of rotation rates and why slow pairs run into unseen angles past L_train — the shared skeleton of the whole back half; (2) rope-scaling-methods [P1] a comparative panel of PI/NTK/YaRN as three ways to reshape that ladder, targeting the most interview-hot and confusing material; (3) position-injection-sites [P2] a data-path map of Approach A vs B with RoPE as the hybrid, upgrading the chapter's ASCII organizing spine. All three are static because a single frame fully conveys each; none require animation.
